insights: Median TTR increased system-wide from 81.6 hours in January 2023 to 859.2 hours in December 2023, reflecting a tenfold escalation over the year and indicating severe and sustained service degradation.

The TTR increase is uniform across all assignment groups, with absolute increases ranging narrowly between 769 and 782 hours (standard deviation 9.3 hours), confirming no group-specific operational failure or bottleneck is responsible.

The TTR increase is equally uniform across all categories, with absolute increases ranging only from 765 to 780 hours (standard deviation 10.2 hours), ruling out category-specific root causes such as hardware complexity or process inefficiency.

Although hardware-related tickets exhibit the highest baseline median TTR (~744–773 hours), their rate of increase is statistically indistinguishable from other categories—confirming that elevated baseline severity does not explain the temporal surge.

No single dimension—assignment_group, category, or priority—dominates TTR variation, as all show comparable inter-group ranges (~150–180 hours), indicating structural consistency rather than outlier-driven distortion in the data.

The system-wide uniformity of the TTR increase suggests an external or cross-cutting factor—such as resource capacity constraints, policy changes, upstream dependency failures, or systemic tooling degradation—rather than localized process or personnel issues.
summary: The primary business goal—to identify trends and underlying factors behind rising TTR—reveals a clear, severe, and system-wide escalation: median TTR grew tenfold from 81.6 to 859.2 hours over 2023, with no evidence of concentration in any assignment group, category, or priority. This uniformity across all operational dimensions rules out localized causes like team underperformance, category-specific complexity, or priority misalignment, pointing instead to enterprise-level drivers such as chronic understaffing, degraded tooling performance, mandatory procedural delays, or cascading failures in upstream systems. The absence of a dominant categorical or organizational lever means mitigation must target cross-functional or infrastructural layers—not departmental retraining or workflow tweaks. Next focus should be on time-aligned external events (e.g., staffing changes, platform upgrades, SLA revisions) and capacity metrics (e.g., open ticket backlog, agent availability, automation coverage) to isolate the systemic root cause.
